card.intuition: Unlike 0/1 knapsack, each item can be used any number of times. The recurrence becomes dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w - wt[i]] + val[i]) for each item — using dp[w - wt[i]] (already updated this pass) instead of the previous row allows reuse.
card.analogy: An all-you-can-eat buffet where you can take multiple servings of any dish. The goal is to maximise satisfaction without exceeding your caloric budget — you're free to load up on your favourite dish.
card.time_complexity: O(n · W) where n = number of items, W = capacity
when_to_use: Coin change (minimise coins / count ways) — items = coin denominations, reuse allowed
Rod cutting problem — cut a rod into pieces for maximum profit
Any knapsack variant where items can be reused
gotchas: Iterate weight w from low to high (unlike 0/1 knapsack which iterates high to low) to allow reuse within the same pass
1D DP array is sufficient — dp[w] already incorporates reuse from the current pass
If no solution exists (dp[target] still 0 or ∞), handle the base case carefully

exercise.prompt: Implement unbounded knapsack: each item may be used multiple times.
exercise.starter_code:
def unbounded_knapsack(weights, values, capacity):
    """
    weights, values: lists of equal length
    capacity: max weight
    Returns maximum value achievable.
    """
    n  = len(weights)
    dp = [0] * (capacity + 1)

    for w in range(1, capacity + 1):   # iterate capacity low-to-high
        for i in range(n):
            if weights[i] <= w:
                dp[w] = max(dp[w], ___________)
    return dp[capacity]
hints: When item i fits (weights[i] <= w), consider dp[w - weights[i]] + values[i] — this reads from the already-updated dp, allowing item reuse
exercise.test_code:
# Items: weights=[1,3,4,5], values=[1,4,5,7], capacity=7
w=[1,3,4,5]; v=[1,4,5,7]
assert unbounded_knapsack(w, v, 7) == 9   # use item 1 (w=3,v=4) twice + item 0 = 4+4+1=9? No: 3+3+1=7 -> 4+4+1=9 ✓
assert unbounded_knapsack(w, v, 0) == 0
assert unbounded_knapsack([2],[5], 6) == 15  # 3 copies of weight-2 item
assert unbounded_knapsack([3],[4], 5) == 4   # only one fits
print('PASS')
